coredata.dc:title: High electrochemical response of TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites as electrode modifiers for Pb (II) detection
coredata.dc:creator: Arham, Zul
Kurniawan, Kurniawan
Anhusadar, Laode
coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of new modifiers based on nanomaterials as Pb (II) sensing electrode composites has attracted much attention in recent years. Modifiers are believed to produce stable electrodes with good sensitivity. In this study, we synthesized TiO2-incorporated carbon dots (TiO2@C-dots) and tested their performance as modifiers in the detection of Pb (II) using a carbon paste electrode (CPE). The fabrication of TiO2@C-dots-decorated CPE (CPE/TiO2@C-dots) has been carried out using several methods including electrochemical, hydrothermal impregnation, and mechanical homogenization at low temperatures. Based on the results of SEM, XRD, and FTIR characterization, the morphology of the nanocomposites is known to consist of uniform solid particles. Also, there are voids on the nanocomposite surface. The nanocomposite has an average particle size of 22.61 nm and a specific 2θ angle of 26.33°. The nanocomposites actively absorb IR at specific wavenumbers of 3600 cm−1 (-OH stretch) and 1759 cm−1 (CO stretch). Performance test results for Pb (II) detection showed that decorative modifiers (TiO2@C-dots) and CPE affected the peak redox current of Pb (II). This effect correlates with the mass of TiO2@C-dots, with a mass of 10.0 mg being the best mass for TiO2@C-dots. During the detection process, Pb (II) underwent redox reactions with oxidation (Epa) and reduction (Epc) potential values of −0.38 V and −0.50 V, respectively. During application, it is controlled by the interaction diffusivity with the Pb (II) molecule, yielding an accuracy of 0.36%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanomaterial
CPE
TiO2@C-dots
Pb (II)
Voltammetry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Performance test of CPE/TiO2@C-dots by voltammetry
The mass effect of TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites
Limit of detection test and limit of quantitation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of working electrode body and design of CPE/TiO2@C-dots performance test by Voltammetry.
Results of SEM-EDS TiO2@C-dots analysis: (a) morphology; (b) histogram of morphology size diameter, and (c) elemental and oxide composition.
Analysis of CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposite: (a) morphology by SEM; (b) diffractogram by XRD, and functional groups by FTIR.

[A] Cyclic voltammograms of pristine CPE in 0.10 M HCl supporting electrolyte; [B] Cyclic voltammograms of performance of the pristine CPE (black line) and CPE/TiO2@C-dots (red line) in Pb (II) detection.
Cyclic voltammograms of oxidation and reduction of Pb (II) on the surface of CPE/TiO2@C-dots at different concentrations.
Effect of TiO2@C-dots mass on peak current in Pb (II) 10.0 mM (supporting electrolyte: 0.10 M HCl).
[A] Effect of scan rate variations on Pb (II) peak current; [B] Plot of root scan rate (υ1/2) vs peak current.
Effect of interfering ion on the peak current of Pb (II).
Histogram of repeatability detection of Pb (II) in the presence of simultaneous interfering Cu2+ and Zn2+ ions.
Plot of Pb (II) concentration vs Ipc (inset image: cyclic voltammogram of the cathodic peak current at various concentrations).
Percentage values of EDS analysis, both elements and oxides from TiO2@C-dots.
The average diameter of CPE/TiO2@C-dots is based on the Scherrer equation.
Comparison of peak redox current values of Pb (II) between CPE and CPE/TiO2@C-dots.
The current and potential peak values of Pb (II) in the interfering test.
Comparison of previous detection results for Pb (II) based on voltammetry.

head.ce:title: High electrochemical response of TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites as electrode modifiers for Pb (II) detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Faculty of Tarbiyah, Institut Agama Islam Negeri (IAIN) Kendari, 93116, Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mathematics and Natural Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mathematics and Natural Sciences, Faculty of Tarbiyah, Institut Agama Islam Negeri (IAIN) Kendari, 93116, Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Textile Chemistry, Politeknik STTT Bandung, 40272, West Java, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Textile Chemistry, Politeknik STTT Bandung, 40272, West Java, Indonesia.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Early Childhood Education, Faculty of Tarbiyah, Institut Agama Islam Negeri (IAIN) Kendari, 93116, Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Early Childhood Education, Faculty of Tarbiyah, Institut Agama Islam Negeri (IAIN) Kendari, 93116, Southeast Sulawesi, Indonesia.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of new modifiers based on nanomaterials as Pb (II) sensing electrode composites has attracted much attention in recent years. Modifiers are believed to produce stable electrodes with good sensitivity. In this study, we synthesized TiO2-incorporated carbon dots (TiO2@C-dots) and tested their performance as modifiers in the detection of Pb (II) using a carbon paste electrode (CPE). The fabrication of TiO2@C-dots-decorated CPE (CPE/TiO2@C-dots) has been carried out using several methods including electrochemical, hydrothermal impregnation, and mechanical homogenization at low temperatures. Based on the results of SEM, XRD, and FTIR characterization, the morphology of the nanocomposites is known to consist of uniform solid particles. Also, there are voids on the nanocomposite surface. The nanocomposite has an average particle size of 22.61 nm and a specific 2θ angle of 26.33°. The nanocomposites actively absorb IR at specific wavenumbers of 3600 cm−1 (-OH stretch) and 1759 cm−1 (CO stretch). Performance test results for Pb (II) detection showed that decorative modifiers (TiO2@C-dots) and CPE affected the peak redox current of Pb (II). This effect correlates with the mass of TiO2@C-dots, with a mass of 10.0 mg being the best mass for TiO2@C-dots. During the detection process, Pb (II) underwent redox reactions with oxidation (Epa) and reduction (Epc) potential values of −0.38 V and −0.50 V, respectively. During application, it is controlled by the interaction diffusivity with the Pb (II) molecule, yielding an accuracy of 0.36%.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal contamination such as lead (Pb (II)) is one of many types of pollutants in the environment. Its presence has attracted the attention of governments and environmental monitoring agencies. Its exposure through the human body disrupts the nervous system and hemoglobin biosynthesis [1]. It accumulates and gradually affects health problems such as brain damage, reproduction, blood pressure, kidney damage, nervous, and anemia. A minimum acceptable limit for Pb (II) in water was set to 10–15 μg/L [2,3].

Spectroscopic-based analytical methods, such as atomic absorption (AAS) and Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) are commonly used to detect Pb (II). Several spectroscopic methods that have been reported are Inductively coupled plasma with optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) [4], Inductively coupled plasma with mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [5,6], hydride generation atomic absorption spectroscopy (HGAAS) [7,8], graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy [7], Electrothermal Atomic Spectroscopy Absorption (ETAAS) [9], and Flame atomic absorption spectrometry (FAAS) [10,11]. However, it is reported to have drawbacks, such as non-portable devices, complex device operating systems, long service life, and high cost of measurement [12,13]. Another widely reported method is the electrochemical method. Based on its application and development, it offers many advantages over spectroscopy. For example, it includes a simple detection process with low measurement costs and a simple instrument design that can be used for field detection. In addition, it offers good accuracy and precision for analytes.

Currently, many electrochemical-based detection techniques have been reported, including voltammetry. Voltammetry has been described as a possible measurement method for detecting Pb (II) [14–17]. The accuracy and precision of this technique are highly influenced by the type of working electrode. The working electrode initiates a reduction-oxidation (redox) reaction, which translates into concentration. Types of working electrodes reported for Pb(II) detection include graphene electrodes [15,17], glassy carbon electrodes [18,19], carbon paste electrodes [20–23], bismuth electrodes [24–26], and gold electrodes [27]. The results show that the electrode performance is affected by the modifier. The addition of modifiers accelerates electron transfer across the electrode surface, resulting in higher peak currents. Modifiers also increase the selectivity of the working electrode. Modifiers synthesized and reported to function include 5-mercapto-1-methyltetrazol grafted on hexagonal mesoporous silica [28], composites consisting of Co3O4 nanoparticles, reduced graphene oxide and chitosan [29], metal-organic framework (Ca-MOF) [30], Au–Bi bimetallic nanoparticles (rGO/Au-Bi) [15], Bimetallic oxides (Fe2O3/Bi2O3) [17], polyrutin/silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) [31], and others [32,33]. Besides that, a new functional nanomaterial that can be applied as a modifier is the TiO2/CNNS composite. TiO2/CNNS is reported to have good electrostatic attraction and large surface area [34].

The synthesis of new modifiers for Pb (II) detection has received considerable attention from researchers. This is aimed at obtaining stable electrodes for environments with high matrix turbulence. The synthesis of the modifier was also due to the finding that the concentration of Pb (II) in water was very low, namely <20 μg/L. This drives the need for sensitive electrodes with low detection limits [1,28,29]. Based on this issue, we report the performance of nanocomposite TiO2@C-dots as modifiers for Pb (II) detection using CPE. CPE is a type of working electrode that has been widely reported for its use in the development of voltammetry-based contaminant analysis [35]. Interest in CPE stems from easily modifiable and economical materials. On the other hand, CPE has low sensitivity and selectivity, and a low electron transfer rate [36,37]. So for continuous applications, the CPE will continue to change and report on its performance. One of the reported modified materials is based on nanomaterials [38,39].

The use of TiO2@C-dots as CPE modifiers in Pb (II) detection has not been reported. TiO2@C-dots have been the focus of our research over the past year due to the unique properties of this material, such as a large surface area so that the interaction between the electrode and the analyte and the current response of the electrode can be increased. Some researchers have reported about TiO2@C-dots, but the novelty of the study we report lies in the application. Previous studies have focused on investigating the role of TiO2@C-dots as photocatalytic materials for decomposing or removing contaminants from both organic and inorganic materials [40–44]. Reported in this work is the role of TiO2@C-dots as electrode modifiers in voltammetry-based environmental contaminant detection systems such as Pb (II).

The results reported in this work are a continuation of previous work. A previous study reported the performance of the modifier TiO2@C-dots in the Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− solution system [36]. In particular, testing this system is the first step in investigating the electrochemical properties of new electrodes. The results show that TiO2@C-dots can increase the electrochemical response of CPE. This increase is seen in the increase in peak redox current, indicating an improved electron transfer process on the electrode surface.

TiO2@C-dots were synthesized by modifying our previous working method [36]. C-dots were obtained by electrolysis of graphite rod electrodes (size ± 0.30 cm, Merck, CAS: 7782-42-5) with a potential bias of 12 V, then taken as much as 2.0 mL and poured into a beaker. Then 20 mL of deionized water and 3 mL of ethanol (Merck, CAS: 64-17-5) were added to the beaker. The mixture was homogenized with a magnetic stirrer and slowly added 0.80 g of TiO2 (Merck, EMSURE: 1.008.08). Stirring was continued at room temperature for 5 h. It was then filtered and heated in an OGS-60 Thermo Scientific oven at a constant temperature of 140 °C for 4 h and continued heating at 60 °C for 12 h.

Synthesis of CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites was carried out by mechanical stirring at 80 °C. In summary, 0.70 g of graphite powder (Merck, CAS: 7782-42-5), 0.30 g of paraffin oil (Merck, CAS: 8042-47-5), and 10.0 mg of TiO2@C-dots were slowly poured onto the watch glass surface. A watch glass containing the nanocomposite was heated to 80 °C using a hotplate. The three components were then rapidly mixed until completely homogeneous and pasty. In this process, we varied the mass of TiO2@C-dots, namely 10, 20, and 30 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance test of CPE/TiO2@C-dots by voltammetry
ce:section.ce:para: The nanocomposite paste was inserted into the working electrode body in the form of a tube with a hole diameter of 3.0 mm. Previously, a copper wire (size ±1.5 mm) was inserted into the working electrode body [1]. For voltammetric measurements, a DY2100B potentiostat with a three-electrode system was used: the working electrode (CPE/TiO2@C-dots), the reference electrode (Ag/AgCl), and the auxiliary electrode (Pt wire). The three electrodes were inserted into a voltammetric cell containing 10.0 mM Pb (II) analyte solution and 0.1 M HCl solution as supporting electrolyte. The Pb (II) solution used originates from Pb(NO3)2 powder (Merck, CAS: 10099-74-8). The potential range used during the measurement is 0.0 to −1.0 V. Fig. 1 shows the structure of the working electrode body used and the CPE/TiO2@C-dots performance test by Voltammetry.

Morphological characterization of CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites using SEM-EDS (JEOL-JSM-6510LV), characterization of crystallinity and average grain size using XRD (Philips: Cu Kα radiation, = 0.154 nm), and functional groups characterization using an IRprestige-21 Shimadzu Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer.

The properties of CPE/TiO2@C-dots were analyzed by SEM, XRD, and FTIR, as shown in Fig. 2 . SEM analysis compared the morphology of TiO2@C-dots with CPE/TiO2@C-dots. The morphology of TiO2@C-dots (Fig. 2a) is characterized by uniform size spherical particles. Diameter morphology analysis using ImageJ software shown in Fig. 2b strengthens the information that the particle size of TiO2@C-dots is uniform with values ranging from 100 to 300 nm (R2 = 0.99). A literature study revealed that the spherical particles were TiO2 particles. The incorporation of C-dots did not change the morphology of TiO2. However, it is uniformly distributed on the TiO2 surface. This situation occurs because the C-dot particles are very small in size. If the particle size is reported to be less than 10 nm [45]. The existence of C-dot particles as a result of incorporation on the surface of TiO2 was demonstrated by the results of EDS analysis (Fig. 2c). The results show that there is a uniform distribution of carbon (C) with 12.03%. In addition, other elements analyzed by EDS were titanium (Ti) and oxygen (O) with percentages of 35.23 (±0.05) and 52.74 (±0.52). The presence of these three elements indicated the successful synthesis of C-dots and TiO2@C-dots. Table 1 shows the percentage values of EDS analysis, both elemental and oxide from nanocomposites.

In the process of mixing CPE and TiO2@C-dots, the CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites are produced in a fixed morphology and mixed homogeneously (Fig. 3 a). After mixing, the TiO2@C-dots particles were indicated by yellow circular lines. Another result obtained from the morphology of the CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposite is the presence of vacancies on their surface. Literature comparison results explain that these gaps are a specific form of carbon paste [46]. The homogeneity of the CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites was confirmed by XRD characterization results (Fig. 3b). The diffractogram shows that the decoration process of CPE and TiO2@C-dots doesn't cause any shift in either 2θ value. Both have specific 2θ values. Refer to JCPDS data for pristine carbon (no. 00-008-0415), CPE has specific 2θ values: 26.42o, 42.36o, 44.46o, 54.51o, 59.98o, and 77.40o. For TiO2, based on JCPDS data no. 01-071-1168, we show that the specific 2θ values of pristine TiO2 are 25.20o, 37.52o, 47.87o, and 54.86o. The 2θ value of the diffractogram at 26.33o is the focus of attention due to its high intensity. Results of previous studies indicated that the value of 2θ = 26.33o is a specific value for the C element. It is expressed as a pristine carbon 2θ value based on JCPDS data no. 00-008-0415, which is 26.42o. This suggests that the combination of C elements between the CPE and C-dots is responsible for the high diffractogram intensity. The 2θ values of 42.27o, 54.45o, and 77.32o are specific values for CPE [30,40]. Specific 2θ values for the TiO2@C-dots are indicated at 2θ of 25.12o, and 47.73o. Previously, these results were reported by Ref. [36]. The inset image shows that the diffractogram at 2θ of 25.1o is in the form of a hump with low intensity. These results illustrate that the nanocomposite is amorphous, causing a low diffractogram intensity. This property comes from TiO2@C-dots.

Calculation of the average diameter of the nanocomposite uses the Debye-Scherrer equation (D = 0.9λ/(FWHM Cos θ)), where D is the crystalline size, λ is the wavelength of X-ray radiation, FWHM is the full width at half maximum in radians. This equation has been used by Refs. [47–50]. Based on the calculation results, the average diameter of CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposite particles is 22.61 nm (Table 2 ). This diameter is smaller than that of TiO2@C-dots reported in a previous study [36]. The change in diameter reveals that the synthesis process produces uniform nanocomposites. Carbon particles and TiO2 NPs are uniformly distributed.

In general, FTIR characterization provides information on functional group absorption occurring at specific wavenumbers [32,33]. Based on Fig. 3c, the absorption of functionalized CPE/TiO2@C-dots occurs at wavenumbers of 3600 cm−1 (-OH stretch), 2881 cm−1 (C–H stretch), 2381 cm−1, and 2289 cm−1 (CC stretch), 1759 cm−1 (CO stretch), 1510 cm−1 (C–C stretch), 995 cm−1 and 852 cm−1 (C–H bend). The –OH stretching vibration occurring at a wavenumber of 3600 cm−1 is believed to originate from the H2O molecule bound to carboxyl groups [38]. This is supported by several previous studies on the chemical structures of both CPE and C-dots. Vibrations at wavenumbers 2289.50 cm−1, 1759.08 cm−1, and 852.54 cm−1 indicate the specific IR absorption of the carbon paste. The vibration at wave number 995 cm−1 is the specific absorption of the TiO2@C-dots. This vibration was shifted by the nanocomposite synthesis process [36].

The effect of the TiO2@C-dots modifiers on the performance of CPE in Pb (II) detection was observed by comparing the performance of two working electrodes, pristine CPE and CPE/TiO2@C-dots. This effect is shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4A is the cyclic voltammogram of pristine CPE in an electrolyte solution containing 0.10 M HCl. Electrolyte solution plays a role in preventing migration currents generated due to differences in charge on the surface of the electrode and the body of the solution. Besides that, it will increase the reactivity of an electrode [51]. The ionic strength of a supporting electrolyte becomes an important part of the electrostatic interactions that occur during the electrochemical process. So the selection of electrolyte concentration is an important consideration. This is our reason for using a supporting electrolyte concentration of 0.10 M. In addition to the type and concentration of the electrolyte, the acidity of the electrolyte solution will affect the peak voltammetric current. Previous research revealed that a low pH is an optimum pH in the detection of Pb (II) by voltammetry. At high pH conditions, a metal hydroxide solution is formed, so that the peak current decreases and the peak shape becomes damaged. In contrast, Fig. 3B (blue line) shows two peak currents, an oxidation (Ipa) current, and a reduction (Ipc) current, which are reversible. This peak was observed when 10 mM Pb (II) was added to the supporting electrolyte solution. This current peak occurs at oxidation potential (Ea) and reduction potential (Ec) of −0.38 V and −0.50 V, respectively. Based on this, we believe that this peak is a current peak resulting from the oxidation and reduction of Pb (II) on the pristine CPE surface.

The effect of the TiO2@C-dots modifier on the detection of Pb (II) is indicated by a significant increase in peak current, as shown in Fig. 4B (red line). Modifiers effectively improve the electrochemical performance of pristine CPE. In the presence of these modifiers, the conductivity properties and electron transfer kinetics of pristine CPE can be successfully modified. Table 3 shows the current and redox potential values of Pb (II) before and after adding TiO2@C-dots. The difference between the resulting Ea and Ec values explains the non-Nernstian redox reactions occurring on both the pristine CPE and CPE/TiO2@C-dots surfaces. This is indicated by the value of ΔEp ≠ 59 mV.

Fig. 5 shows cyclic voltammograms of oxidation and reduction of Pb (II) on the surface of CPE/TiO2@C-dots with various concentrations. This test aims to obtain information regarding the peak current of Pb (II) described previously in Fig. 4B. Based on the voltammogram, the peak value of the redox current increased as the concentration of the Pb (II) solution in the supporting electrolyte solution increased. This indicates a linear relationship between the concentration of Pb (II) and the resulting current for both oxidation and reduction. The currents are generated at the same potential, as shown in Fig. 4B. These results reinforce the information that the peak currents at potentials of −0.38 V and −0.50 V are the peak currents of Pb (II).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mass effect of TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The change in the mass of the modifier TiO2@C-dots used in the preparation of CPE/TiO2@C-dots was observed for its effect on the detection of Pb (II). The modifier masses used were 10.0 mg, 30.0 mg, and 50.0 mg. This bulk selection builds on previously reported work. Previous studies on aqueous Fe(CN)6 3−/Fe(CN)6 4− systems showed that the mass of TiO2@C-dots affects the performance of pristine CPE. Fig. 6 shows the cyclic voltammograms of the effect of mass change of TiO2@C-dots on the performance of pristine CPE. A mass of 10.0 mg is the optimal mass for using TiO2@C-dots as a modifier for Pb (II) detection. Using large masses (30 mg and 50 mg), the pristine CPE performance experiences a decrease in peak currents for both oxidation and reduction. This phenomenon is closely related to the conductivity of the electrodes and the surface of the resulting nanocomposites [36,52]. Adding other modifiers can reduce the electrode conductivity and increase the electrode surface area. This condition degrades the current response [53].

Experiments with different scan rates are used to explain whether the electrochemical reactions are driven by diffusion or other processes such as migration and convection. Fig. 7 shows the voltammograms of the effect of the scan rate difference on the peak current of Pb (II). Fig. 7A shows how the scan rate relates to the peak current of Pb (II) on the CPE/TiO2@C-dots surface. Based on the figure, it can be seen that both the oxidation and reduction peak currents increase as the scan rate value is varied from 10 to 500 mVs−1. This condition describes a linear relationship between scan rate and peak current. A potential root plot of scan rate versus peak current (Fig. 7B) obtained the linear regression equation y = −0.0897x - 0.228 with R2 = 0.98 (for Ipa) and y = 0.0558x + 0.0753 with R2 = 0.99 (for Ipc). This explains that the redox processes occurring on the surface of the electrode are controlled by the diffusion rate [36,37,46]. This rate is identical to the difference in the concentration of Pb (II) at the surface of the electrode and the body of the solution. In addition, the regression equation can explain the electrochemically active surface area (EASA) of Pb (II) on the surface of CPE/TiO2@C-dots. AESA is determined using the Randles-Sevcik equation, Ip= (2.69 × 05) n3/2AC D1/2υ1/2. Based on the slope value of Ipa, information is obtained that the EASA value of Pb (II) is 0.17 cm2.

Interfering ion tests are very important in knowing the performance of CPE/TiO2@C-dots. This concerns the application of CPE/TiO2@C-dots as new electrode candidates in the development of voltammetry-based Pb (II) detection. Fig. 8 shows how the response generated by the electrode is in detecting Pb (II) when interfering ions are added to the test solution. Adding 1.0 mM Cu2+ ions (red line) increase the peak current of Pb (II). In addition, the peak current has a potential value shift. Similar to Cu2+ ions, the addition of Zn2+ ions (blue line) also increases the peak current and shifts the potential value. An increase in the peak current value of Pb (II) was also seen when the test was performed simultaneously with two interfering ions, namely Cu2+ and Zn2+ (green line). Nevertheless, this test shows that the presence of either one or two interfering ions leads to a constant addition of Pb (II) peak current levels. From this, we conclude that the CPE/TiO2@C-dots are very stable despite the presence of some interfering ions in the test solution. Table 4 shows the current and potential peak levels of Pb (II) in this test.

Fig. 9 shows the histogram of the repeatability test of the nanocomposite CPE/TiO2@C-dots. The test aims to confirm the accuracy of the nanocomposites in detecting Pb (II) in the presence of 1.0 mM of the interfering ions Cu2+ and Zn2+ respectively. Based on the histogram, 20 replicates yield a relative standard deviation of 0.63%. This value was obtained at a Pb (II) concentration of 10 mM. Based on these values, the accuracy level of CPE/TiO2@C-dots is found to be very good [54,55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Limit of detection test and limit of quantitation
ce:section.ce:para: The limit of detection (LOD) test and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) in principle have different purposes in an analytical test. LOD aims to get an overview of a concentration that can be detected. LOQ is used to obtain a detectable quantitation limit. In this work, the determination of LOD and LOQ will describe the sensitivity of CPE/TiO2@C-dots in the detection of Pb (II) by cyclic voltammetry. The calculations of both refer to the regression equation shown in Fig. 10 . Where the varied range of Pb (II) concentrations produces a slope value of 2 × 10−5 μM (R2 = 0.9966). Based on the slope values, the LOD and LOQ values obtained were 0.0441 μM and 0.0588 μM, respectively. These results describe the sensitivity of CPE/TiO2@C-dots in the detection of Pb (II) by cyclic voltammetry. However, this sensitivity can be increased through the application of other voltammetric techniques such as stripping voltammetry. This technique is seen as a reliable technique in the detection of trace metals due to the pre-concentration process on the surface of the working electrode. Table 5 shows a comparison of the results of previous detections of Pb (II).

In this work, the performance of CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites as new electrodes in the voltammetric detection of Pb (II) has been demonstrated. As a modifier, TiO2@C-dots play an important role in improving CPE performance. This role can be seen by the increase in the peak electrochemical currents of Pb (II), with values of −0.38 V and −0.50 V at both anodic and cathodic potentials, respectively. The high current peak is affected by the mass of TiO2@C-dots. In this case, the optimal mass for using TiO2@C-dots as CPE modifiers is 10 mg. During the tests, the interaction of Pb (II) on the surface of the CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites was controlled by the diffusion rate. Other results show that the nanocomposite has excellent accuracy in detecting Pb (II) with a % RSD value of 0.63%. Overall, these results support the further development of the CPE/TiO2@C-dots nanocomposites as voltammetric Pb (II) sensing electrodes.

Zul Arham: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Kurniawan Kurniawan: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Laode Anhusadar: Software, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced-charge transfer over molecularly imprinted polyaniline modified graphene/TiO2 nanocomposite electrode for highly selective detection of fipronil insecticide
Mater. Sci. Semicond. Process.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Nurdin, Z. Arham, W.O. Irna, M. Maulidiyah, K. Kurniawan, I. Irwan, A.A. Umar, Enhanced-charge transfer over molecularly imprinted polyaniline modified graphene/TiO2 nanocomposite electrode for highly selective detection of fipronil insecticide, Mater. Sci. Semicond. Process. 151 (2022) 106994.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel analytical method for sensitive determination of lead: hydrogen assisted T-shape slotted quartz tube-atom trap-flame atomic absorption spectrometry
Microchem. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Uslu, C. Buyukpinar, T. Unutkan, H. Serbest, S.A.N. Nevin, F. Turak, S. Bakirdere, A novel analytical method for sensitive determination of lead: Hydrogen assisted T-shape slotted quartz tube-atom trap-flame atomic absorption spectrometry, Microchem. J. 137 (2018) 155-159.
